notes.anchor: In the consulship of Lucius Julius Caesar and Gaius Marcius Figulus
notes.note: Romans dated the year by its two ordinary consuls; this is 64 BC. The solemn consular formula opening the letter underlines that Cicero is recording his son's birth as a public, datable event. This L. Iulius Caesar (cos. 64) is not the future dictator.

notes.anchor: I have been increased by a little son
notes.note: The birth of Marcus Tullius Cicero the younger, Cicero's only son, in 65 BC (the parallel Latin dates it by the consuls of 64; the editorial tradition places the letter in 65). 'Terentia safe' reports that his wife came through the delivery well.

Catiline, a rival for the consulship of 63, was on trial for extortion (repetundae) over his governorship of Africa. Cicero weighed defending him to win his goodwill in the canvass; in the event he did not, and Catiline was acquitted by other means.

notes.anchor: We have judges such as we wished, with the highest goodwill of the prosecutor
notes.note: A frank admission that the jury panel was favourably composed and the prosecutor (P. Clodius) collusively lenient --- the kind of arranged trial (praevaricatio) common in extortion cases. Cicero expected an acquittal.

The nobiles --- the entrenched office-holding aristocracy, with whom Atticus was on easy terms --- were expected to resist a 'new man' reaching the consulship. Cicero wants Atticus back in Rome to help win them over.
Honos here is the consulship itself, the highest 'honour' (office) in the Roman cursus honorum. Cicero fears the nobility will block his bid for it.
